title: signalDrain double-decrements queueDepth: once for the drain signal and again for each resolved waiter, causing queueDepth to go below the true in-flight count
description: waitForDrain increments queueDepth before deciding whether to block (line 680), so each pending waiter already occupies one slot in the depth count. signalDrain correctly decrements once at line 692 for the chunk that the worker just consumed. Then inside the while-loop at line 701 it decrements again for every waiter it resolves, but those waiters correspond to pushes whose increments are still counted in queueDepth (their chunks have not been sent yet). This makes queueDepth go negative (floored at 0), causing the adaptive HWM to appear wider than configured: subsequent waitForDrain calls see a depth lower than reality and do not block when they should. Under sustained backpressure the worker queue grows beyond the configured HWM, defeating the backpressure contract.
evidence: Lines 692 and 701 of scheduler.ts both decrement queueDepth. The first decrement (line 692) accounts for the chunk the worker just drained. The second decrement inside the while-loop (line 701) fires for each resolved waiter, but each waiter's push already incremented queueDepth at line 680 — those increments are still live because the resolved pushes haven't been consumed by the worker yet. Under burst traffic this drives queueDepth toward zero prematurely, allowing more pushes than the HWM permits.

title: pool.park then pool.unpark called back-to-back on the victim worker, immediately cancelling the parked state before assignWorker uses it
description: After the preemption ack resolves with 'paused', the scheduler records the victim's decoder as parked by calling pool.park(backgroundWorker) and sets workerPausedSession. On the very next line pool.unpark(backgroundWorker) removes the worker from the parked set (pool.ts line 276: parked.delete). The worker's activeSessionId is then manually set to RESERVED_SESSION_ID so assignWorker/bind can proceed. The pool.assertInvariants check in DEV mode will not fire here because park/unpark are called but pool.bind is not — however parkedCount will read 0 even though the logical 'paused session on worker' concept is live. The park/unpark pair achieves nothing and leaves the worker in an inconsistent state: it is in neither idle nor active nor parked, so pool.size accounts for it but no set tracks it between unpark and the subsequent pool.bind inside assignWorker.
evidence: Lines 952-953 of scheduler.ts call park() then unpark() back-to-back. park() moves the worker to the parked set (active.delete, idle.delete, parked.add, activeSessionId=null). unpark() immediately calls parked.delete, leaving the worker in workers map but absent from idle/active/parked sets — a transient limbo. The subsequent line 954 sets activeSessionId=RESERVED_SESSION_ID manually, bypassing normal reserve(). The park/unpark round-trip is functionally a no-op that transiently violates pool set invariants and confuses assertInvariants in DEV mode (parked shows 0 while workerPausedSession has an entry).

title: On dedupe promotion the promoted record's state is mutated before the counter adjustment reads it, so the wrong counter gets incremented
description: When cancelling a primary with surviving subscribers, the code transfers worker/paused/queued state to promotedRecord (lines 541-560), which sets promotedRecord.state to 'running'/'paused'/'queued'. Then at line 572 it checks isSubscriber to do the promoted record's counter book-keeping. At line 575 it reads promotedRecord.state to decide which counter to increment — but if the transfer block set promotedRecord.state to 'paused' (line 550) or 'queued' (line 555), those new values are used. Subscriber records are created with state='running' (line 322 in acquireSlot), so a subscriber that was never reassigned still has state='running'. The paused transfer (line 550) correctly changes it, but if the worker transfer path (lines 541-547) was taken, promotedRecord.state stays 'running' which is correct. The queued transfer sets state='queued' (line 555) so _queuedCount gets incremented, but the subscriber was previously counted in _subscriberCount not _runningCount — so _runningCount is never decremented for the old subscriber entry yet _queuedCount is incremented, overcounting by 1.
evidence: Lines 553-560 set promotedRecord.state='queued' before the isSubscriber block at line 572 reads promotedRecord.state. A subscriber record always starts with state='running' (line 320), so before mutation _subscriberCount tracked it — but _runningCount did not. After mutation the block decrements _subscriberCount (correct) and increments _queuedCount (correct for the new state) but never increments then decrements _runningCount. The net effect is _queuedCount overcount by 1 and _runningCount undercount by 0 for the queued-transfer path — _runningCount is correct only because subscribers were never in _runningCount, but _queuedCount gains one phantom entry.

title: workerId returned for a dedupe subscriber is -1 when primaryRecord has no worker yet (e.g. primary is still queued), but callers may treat -1 as a valid worker ID
description: When a dedupe subscriber joins and the primary session is still in the 'queued' state (no worker assigned yet), acquireSlot returns { workerId: -1 }. The expression is primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1. The calling code in jxl-session presumably treats the workerId as informational, but if any caller branches on workerId !== -1 to decide whether the decode has started, it will incorrectly believe the decode has not started even after the primary gets a worker. There is no follow-up notification to subscribers when the primary transitions from queued to running, so the -1 is silently stale.
code_excerpt: return { workerId: primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1 };
evidence: Line 334 confirms the -1 sentinel is returned when the primary has no worker. contracts-1 and contracts-2 flag the same pattern from a different angle. The -1 is a documented gap: no follow-up message is sent when the primary transitions from queued to running, so the subscriber's workerId is permanently stale for queued primaries.

title: waitForDrain blocks when queueDepth >= hwm but the condition is < hwm (return early), meaning the blocking threshold is hwm not hwm-1, so HWM is effectively 1 less than configured
description: waitForDrain increments queueDepth then returns immediately if queueDepth < hwm, queuing a waiter otherwise. This means the Nth push (where N == hwm) blocks rather than being allowed through. For example with pushHwm=4 and adaptiveHwm()=4, the 4th push will see queueDepth==4 which is NOT < 4, so it blocks. Effectively only hwm-1 pushes are in-flight at a time. The comment says 'Backpressure high-water mark: pushes after this depth block' which implies pushes AT this depth should also be allowed. Whether this is a spec bug or implementation bug depends on intent, but the mismatch between the name 'high-water mark' (typically the maximum allowed before blocking) and the < comparison (blocks at == hwm) means actual capacity is pushHwm-1.
code_excerpt:
bp.queueDepth++;
if (bp.queueDepth < this.adaptiveHwm()) return;  // blocks when >= hwm, not > hwm
evidence: Line 681 uses strict < so queueDepth==hwm does not return early and falls through to create a waiter. The N-th push (N=hwm) blocks instead of flowing through, meaning effective in-flight capacity is hwm-1. This is either an intentional 'strictly less than' semantics or an off-by-one; either way it differs from the typical HWM definition and from the adaptiveHwm comment which scales around pushHwm as the neutral point.

title: In tryPreempt's 'terminal' branch the victim session is already cleaned up by handleWorkerMessage, but tryPreempt does not decrement _runningCount or update the victim record before acquiring the new worker
description: When the victim emits a terminal message (decode_final etc.) during the preemption window, handleWorkerMessage fires first: it calls cleanupSession(victimSessionId) and releases the worker to the pool. The ack handler then resolves with resolvedKind='terminal'. In the terminal branch (line 905), tryPreempt calls pool.acquire() and then assignWorker. By this point cleanupSession has already run for the victim, so the session is fully cleaned up. However preemptionCount is incremented (line 914), which is fine. The concern is that if pool.acquire() returns null here (pool is contested), the function returns null — but the visible session that caused the preemption attempt is not re-queued. The acquireSlot caller then receives null from tryPreempt and falls through to the queue path, which is correct. This is not a bug by itself but the control flow is subtle and lacks a comment explaining why no re-queue is needed here.
evidence: When tryPreempt returns null at line 916, acquireSlot falls through to the queue path at line 388, which correctly enqueues the session and waits. The session is not lost. The finder correctly identifies the control flow is subtle but explicitly acknowledges 'This is not a bug by itself' — there is no concrete failure scenario, only a documentation gap.

title: adjustSessionCount guards against negative values with Math.max(0,...) but silently masks counter drift rather than asserting in DEV mode
description: The counters _runningCount, _queuedCount, _pausedCount, and _subscriberCount are all clamped to 0 via Math.max rather than asserting. If a double-decrement or missed increment occurs (like the promotion queued-transfer bug in logic-3), the counters silently stabilize at 0 instead of going negative, making getMetrics() return plausible-looking values. Adding a DEV-mode assertion (like pool.ts's assertInvariants pattern) when a counter would go below 0 would catch counter drift bugs at the point of mutation rather than letting them propagate silently to monitoring dashboards.
evidence: adjustSessionCount (lines 806-819) uses Math.max(0,...) for all four counters with no DEV-mode assertion. pool.ts has assertInvariants() (line 650) as a pattern but scheduler.ts has no equivalent. The confirmed logic-3 bug would go undetected in DEV mode because the clamp hides the drift — a DEV assertion would catch it immediately.

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: CoreBudget drainWaiters and the backpressure signalDrain loop share the same ring-buffer compaction pattern but have no shared invariant tests verifying tokens never go negative or exceed capacity after arbitrary acquire/release sequences
description: Both CoreBudget.drainWaiters (budget.ts) and Scheduler.signalDrain (scheduler.ts) implement the same amortised copyWithin compaction pattern. Neither has a property-based test driving arbitrary acquire/release sequences to verify that available tokens stay within [0, capacity] and that waiter FIFO order is preserved. A fast-check or similar property test would have caught the signalDrain double-decrement (logic-1) since it would produce negative effective depths under random burst patterns.
code_excerpt:
// budget.ts drainWaiters uses copyWithin compaction
// scheduler.ts signalDrain uses same pattern with double-decrement bug
evidence: The test directory has no property-based tests for either CoreBudget or signalDrain. The logic-1 double-decrement bug would surface immediately under random burst sequences in a fast-check harness. The opportunity is genuine.

title: __JXL_DEV__ global flag read from globalThis without origin guard, injectable by co-page scripts
description: The DEV flag is computed at module load via `typeof (globalThis as any).__JXL_DEV__ !== 'undefined'`. Any script running in the same browsing context can set `window.__JXL_DEV__ = true` before the module loads and permanently enable verbose console.warn output in production.
evidence: Line 14 of pool.ts confirms the plain globalThis lookup. A co-page script can set window.__JXL_DEV__ = 1 before the module initializes to force DEV mode. The severity is low (no secret leak) but assertInvariants() throws in DEV mode, meaning an attacker can trigger assertion failures that become unhandled rejections in production.

title: sourceKey parameter accepted with no length or content validation, enabling memory pressure via large key strings
description: The `sourceKey` parameter in `acquireSlot` is stored as-is as a Map key in DedupeRegistry.keyToSession and sessionToKey. There is no length cap or character validation.
code_excerpt:
if (params.sourceKey !== null) {
  const primaryId = this.dedupe.findPrimary(params.sourceKey);
evidence: sourceKey is passed by the internal jxl-session layer, not from untrusted external input. The scheduler is not a public HTTP endpoint — it is an internal TypeScript module. The attack vector requires a compromised caller that already has full JS execution context, at which point sourceKey inflation is not the threat model. No concrete attack path exists without prior compromise of the calling layer.

title: CoreBudget allows capacity=0, causing all acquire() calls to throw unconditionally
description: The constructor validates `capacity >= 0` (not `> 0`), so `new CoreBudget(0)` is accepted. Any subsequent `acquire(cost)` where `cost > 0` will throw.
evidence: Lines 17-22 of budget.ts confirm capacity=0 is accepted. Line 36 shows `cost > this.capacity` throws, so any acquire(1) on a capacity=0 budget throws permanently. The globalCoreBudget uses Math.max(1,...) but library consumers passing a custom CoreBudget with navigator.hardwareConcurrency=0 (some environments) would create a broken scheduler with no construction-time diagnostic.

title: No schema validation on acquireSlot parameters at the public API boundary
description: The `acquireSlot` method accepts caller-supplied parameters without runtime type-narrowing validation.
code_excerpt: async acquireSlot(params: { sessionId: string; priority: Priority; ... })
evidence: acquireSlot has no runtime validation beyond the destroyed check. An invalid priority string would flow to PriorityQueue.lane() whose switch has no default handler, causing a silent push to undefined. The opportunity is real and low-effort to address.

title: Pool capacity check and worker acquisition are not atomic: two concurrent acquireSlot calls can both pass tryAcquireIdle/acquire and both get null, or both pass and race to consume the last slot
description: After the admission gate await, two concurrent acquireSlot calls can both call tryAcquireIdle and both get null, then both enter the async spawn path concurrently.
evidence: JavaScript is single-threaded. tryAcquireIdle() is synchronous and atomically removes the worker from idle before returning it (line 522: idle.delete). Two concurrent callers cannot interleave within a single synchronous call. The finder's concern about concurrent awaits is valid in spirit but the pool's spawn counter (spawning++) is incremented synchronously before any await, so totalAllocatedOrSpawning correctly prevents over-spawn. The 'wasted spawn latency' scenario described is a performance concern, not a correctness bug.

title: The async drainQueue IIFE is launched with void and continues running after scheduler.shutdown() resolves, creating a task that can access destroyed scheduler state
description: The async IIFE at line 1363 is started with `void (async () => { ... })()`. When shutdown() is called, an in-flight drainQueue IIFE can continue executing after pool.shutdown() resolves.
evidence: pool.acquire() returns null once destroyed (line 191 of pool.ts: `if (this.destroyed) return null`), so the IIFE will break out of the while loop when pool is destroyed. However, the IIFE's finally block sets drainingQueue=false on the scheduler after sessions.clear() may have run, and the retry setTimeout (line 1393) is not tracked or cancelled on shutdown. Both the dangling finally and the untracked retry timer are real issues.

title: tryPreempt awaits ack from victim but does not re-check if the incoming session was already cancelled during the await window
description: tryPreempt awaits a pause/cancel ack from the victim worker. During this window, params.sessionId could be cancelled. If tryPreempt has already called assignWorker before returning, the session is already bound but cancelSession was already recorded, and the session record created by assignWorker is never cleaned up.
evidence: acquireSlot checks cancelledDuringAcquisition at lines 379-384 after tryPreempt returns, but only if tryPreempt returned non-null. If tryPreempt calls assignWorker (line 974) then returns backgroundWorker.id, then the cancellation check fires and calls abortAcquisition — but assignWorker has already created the session record and bound the worker. abortAcquisition calls dedupe.complete and releaseAdmission but does not send decode_cancel to the already-bound worker, leaving the worker slot occupied indefinitely.

title: recycle() fires cleanupAndRemove() as a void-unawaited promise; budget released before worker terminates
description: recycle() calls `void this.cleanupAndRemove(worker)`. The budget release happens synchronously in cleanupAndRemove, which can unblock a waiting CoreBudget.acquire() before the worker thread has actually terminated.
evidence: cleanupAndRemove (lines 621-643) synchronously removes the worker from all sets and calls coreBudget.release() (line 637) before awaiting handle.shutdown(). A new worker can be spawned immediately after budget release while the old worker is still shutting down. Under a cost=N MT pool this transiently allows N+N active threads until shutdown completes, momentarily exceeding the intended concurrency bound.

category: task leak / resource leak on error path
title: spawn() acquires a CoreBudget token before the spawning counter is incremented; a concurrent shutdown() between acquire() and spawning++ can leave the token permanently consumed
description: In spawn(), the budget is acquired at line 421, and acquiredBudget is set true. The spawning counter is incremented at line 426 only after the budget await resolves.
evidence: The finder's own analysis (final paragraph) traces the destroy path and concludes the budget IS released at line 438 when workers.has() is false. The code correctly handles this case: spawnInner returns without adding to workers when destroyed, then spawn()'s try block checks workers.has and releases the budget. The finder's self-reported confidence is 'low' and the analysis concludes it is correct on careful reading.

title: Pause-path in tryPreempt does pool.park then pool.unpark immediately in sequence, bypassing park's intended lifecycle
description: After a decode_paused ack, pool.park() then pool.unpark() are called back-to-back, leaving the worker in a state not tracked by any pool set.
evidence: This is the same observation as logic-2 but filed as a concurrency concern. Confirmed: the park/unpark ping-pong is a real code smell. The worker is in a limbo state between unpark() and the subsequent pool.bind() inside assignWorker, during which no set tracks it. assertInvariants() (line 650) would flag a worker in workers but not in idle/active/parked if called at this point.

title: If acquireSlot throws after dedupe.register() but before setupSignalAbort(), the abort listener is never registered
description: abortAcquisition() calls dedupe.complete() then throws. The abort listener is only registered by setupSignalAbort() which happens after this path.
evidence: The finder acknowledges this 'appears mostly safe' and is only a concern for future code changes. If abortAcquisition fires before setupSignalAbort, the caller already has a rejected promise (abortAcquisition throws), so they will not wait for the abort listener. No current resource is leaked — the concern is defensive hygiene for future refactors, not a present bug.

title: drainQueue IIFE guard (drainingQueue) can cause a missed wake-up when two drain triggers interleave
description: If an async drainQueue IIFE is in-flight when a new drainQueue call arrives, the new call no-ops, and the queued session must wait until the existing IIFE loop processes it.
evidence: The drain guard at the IIFE start prevents double-entry but also prevents the new trigger from re-driving the queue if the in-flight IIFE breaks out due to pool.acquire() returning null. When the IIFE exits with drainingQueue=false, there is no mechanism to restart it unless another worker completes. A queued session can experience one full worker-lifetime delay as a result.

title: setupSignalAbort has a race: if signal fires between the aborted check and addEventListener, the abort listener is registered but never fires for an already-aborted signal
description: setupSignalAbort checks signal.aborted synchronously then registers addEventListener. If signal.abort() is called in a microtask between the two lines, the listener is registered after the event fired.
evidence: JavaScript is single-threaded. AbortController.abort() is synchronous and the 'abort' event fires synchronously to all registered listeners at the moment abort() is called. Between line 1259 (check) and line 1264 (addEventListener), no other JS can run — both lines execute in the same synchronous block within a microtask. A microtask from another Promise chain cannot interleave within a synchronous sequence. The race described is not possible in the JS event model.

title: spawnStaggered Promise leaks as an unresolved promise when the stagger timer is cleared during shutdown
description: prewarm() calls `void this.spawnStaggered(toSpawn)`. When shutdown() clears the stagger timer, the inner Promise<void> that was awaiting the timer never resolves, leaving spawnStaggered hanging as an unresolved promise.
evidence: shutdownInner (line 349) clears staggerTimer but does not resolve the pending promise. The spawnStaggered async function awaits that promise with no reject path for the timer-cleared case. The promise is abandoned — it is not in spawnPromises and never settles. In practice this is a minor memory leak (the Promise and its closure) but no correctness issue since spawnStaggered is fire-and-forget.

title: CoreBudget.acquire() check-then-decrement is only safe in single-threaded JS; would be TOCTOU in SharedArrayBuffer context
description: acquire() checks tokens then decrements in two statements. Safe in single-threaded JS but would be a TOCTOU if ported to SAB/Atomics.
evidence: The codebase uses standard single-threaded JS. There is no SharedArrayBuffer usage in the scheduler. The finder explicitly states this is not a real race in the current runtime and labels it a 'low-severity awareness item.' No actionable bug exists.

title: acquireSlot has four separate post-await cancelled-guard checks that could be unified
description: acquireSlot() checks cancelledDuringAcquisition and signal.aborted at three separate points, each with the same three-statement pattern.
evidence: The three-check pattern is repeated at lines 345-350, 364-369, and 379-384. A throwIfCancelled() helper would reduce copy-paste and ensure new await points in acquireSlot automatically get the full guard. The opportunity is genuine and relevant given that concurrency-3 confirms a missing cancellation check after tryPreempt's assignWorker call.

category: no backpressure mechanism on producer-consumer path
title: waitForDrain backpressure state does not exist for queued sessions; buffered chunks accumulate unboundedly before worker assignment
description: When a session is queued, send() buffers chunks into bufferedChunks. There is no backpressure for queued sessions — waitForDrain returns immediately because no backpressure record exists.
evidence: waitForDrain at line 673 returns immediately if the session record has no backpressure field, which is the case for all queued sessions. A caller that aggressively sends chunks while waiting for a worker will accumulate unbounded bufferedChunks. This is an existing design limitation acknowledged in the description — backpressure for queued sessions is a non-trivial addition.

title: Preemption timeout catch swallows the error silently after recycling the worker
description: In tryPreempt(), the catch block after the 2-second race catches the preempt-timeout error and recycles backgroundWorker but does not log or surface it.
evidence: Lines 897-901 confirm the bare catch with no logging. A preemption timeout means a worker is unresponsive for 2 seconds and gets recycled — this is a significant operational event that is silently dropped. If pool.recycle() inside the catch throws (theoretically possible if workers.has() is stale), that secondary error is also swallowed.

title: Async drainQueue fires setTimeout without storing the timer handle, preventing cleanup on shutdown
description: In the error-recovery branch of the async drain loop (line 1393), `setTimeout(() => this.drainQueue(), delayMs)` is called with no handle stored, so it cannot be cancelled during scheduler shutdown.
code_excerpt: setTimeout(() => this.drainQueue(), delayMs);
evidence: Line 1393 calls setTimeout with no stored handle. drainQueue() does check this.destroyed at the top and returns early, so the functional impact is minor (one no-op call after shutdown). However across many failures the timers accumulate, and there is no way to cancel them all on shutdown — unlike staggerTimer (line 349) which is explicitly cancelled. The fix is trivial: store the handle and clear it in shutdown.

title: cancelSession promotion path continues if promotedRecord is undefined, silently orphaning the promoted session
description: When the primary is cancelled and a subscriber is promoted, if promotedRecord is undefined (this.sessions.get(promotedTo) returns undefined), all transfers are skipped silently, leaving the old primary's worker/pending/gate unreleased.
evidence: Line 571 calls adjustSessionCount(record, -1) unconditionally. Lines 572-578 use promotedRecord?. optional chaining but if promotedRecord is undefined all counter adjustments for the promoted session are skipped. The promoted session's isSubscriber flag is never cleared, _subscriberCount is never decremented for it, and its worker/gate is never transferred. Under normal operation dedupe.cancelSubscriber returns a promotedTo that always has a session record, but the missing guard is a real correctness assumption without enforcement.

category: retry without backoff cap / wedge risk
title: drainQueue async error path resets drainRetryCount before rejecting the head, allowing infinite retry on the next entry
description: When MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES is exhausted, drainRetryCount is reset to 0 and the failing head is dequeued and rejected. But the next entry also gets MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES attempts.
evidence: Lines 1394-1399 reset drainRetryCount to 0 after exhaustion. With a persistently broken pool (e.g. always-failing spawn), a queue of N entries each gets MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES * exponential backoff (up to 2s per attempt). For 5 retries per entry and N entries, total hang time is O(N * 5 * 2s) = O(10N) seconds before all callers are rejected.

title: tryPreempt: after terminal-ack, pool.acquire() returning null leaves the incoming session falling through to queue path (not a hang)
description: In the resolvedKind === 'terminal' branch, pool.acquire() returning null causes tryPreempt to return null, which falls through to the queue path in acquireSlot.
evidence: The finder's own analysis concludes this path 'is fine.' When tryPreempt returns null, acquireSlot falls through to the queue path at line 388 which correctly enqueues the session. No hang occurs. The finding is a documentation request, not a bug.

title: abortAcquisition dispatches decode_cancelled to subscribers with bare catch {}, silently hiding handler exceptions
description: In abortAcquisition(), subscriber handlers are invoked inside `try { h(...) } catch {}`. Exceptions from handlers are silently dropped.
code_excerpt: try { h({ type: "decode_cancelled", sessionId: sub } as WorkerToMainMessage); } catch {}
evidence: The bare catch{} pattern is consistent throughout the entire codebase (every handler dispatch site uses it) and is documented by the comment 'handler must not throw.' This is an intentional design decision — handlers are user callbacks and the scheduler must not be destabilized by throwing handlers. The finder's self-reported confidence is 'low.' No bug exists.

title: CoreBudget.release() over-release warning fires only in dev mode, with no production-mode guard on token accumulation
description: If release() is called with a very large cost value due to a bug, tokens is clamped to capacity by Math.min, but only a dev-mode warning is emitted. Duplicate releases silently inflate the available count.
evidence: Lines 51-54 of budget.ts confirm the dev-only warning. In production a double-release silently restores capacity to full, potentially allowing more concurrent workers than the budget intends. A production-mode metric increment (no console.warn) would allow monitoring to detect double-release bugs without adding log noise.

title: spawnInner: worker added to pool before wireWorker() runs; if wireWorker throws, worker is in idle set but not wired
description: In spawnInner(), the worker is added to workers and idle at lines 471-472, then wireWorker() is called at line 473. If wireWorker() throws, the worker is already in the idle set in a partially-initialized state.
evidence: wireWorker() only calls worker.handle.onError?.() and worker.handle.onExit?.() — both are optional chained calls that return void. There is no code path in wireWorker that throws. The finder acknowledges this is 'low-risk theoretical' with 'low' confidence. No real failure scenario exists.

title: tryPreempt timeout path synthesizes cancel to victimHandlers from a stale snapshot, races with cleanupSession
description: victimHandlers is captured as a live reference to the handlers array. Between capture and iteration, cleanupSession() is called which may partially tear down the record.
evidence: JS is single-threaded. The handlers array reference is captured synchronously and iterated synchronously before any await. cleanupSession is called after the iteration (line 928), not before or during. No concurrent modification is possible. The 'live reference' concern is only relevant in multi-threaded environments.

title: drainQueue uses console.error for the only error log in the scheduler; no error taxonomy or structured event
description: The entire scheduler has a single console.error at line 1384 and all other error paths are bare catch{} or silent. No structured error taxonomy or error event for callers.
code_excerpt: console.error("[jxl-scheduler] drainQueue error:", err);
evidence: The codebase has exactly one console.error in the scheduler. All other error paths (preemption timeout, handler exceptions) are silently swallowed. An onError callback on Scheduler would allow jxl-session to surface diagnostics without coupling to console, and would help distinguish transient spawn failures from protocol errors.

title: admissionGate.admit() has no timeout; a stalled gate permanently blocks acquireSlot
description: The admissionGate.admit() call is awaited without any timeout. If the AdmissionGate implementation stalls, the acquireSlot Promise never resolves or rejects.
code_excerpt: const release = await this.admissionGate.admit(params.sessionId, params.priority);
evidence: Line 343 awaits admit() with no timeout and no AbortSignal integration. A bug in an AdmissionGate implementation (e.g. semaphore never released) permanently hangs the acquireSlot caller. The contracts-10 finding shows AdmissionGate behavior has zero test coverage, increasing the risk of a stall going undetected.

category: missing null check / potential wrong workerId
title: Dedupe subscriber acquireSlot returns workerId=-1 when primaryRecord has no worker yet
description: When a subscriber dedupes onto a primary session that is still queued, the return value is `{ workerId: -1 }`.
code_excerpt: return { workerId: primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1 };
evidence: Same code location as logic-4 and contracts-1. Confirmed — workerId=-1 is returned for queued primaries and there is no follow-up notification. This finding is a duplicate perspective on the same issue as logic-4/contracts-1/contracts-2.

title: spawn() catch block uses coreBudget non-null assertion (!.) which is fragile
description: In the catch block of spawn() at line 443, `this.coreBudget!.release(actualCost)` uses a non-null assertion. If a future refactor moves the acquiredBudget flag set, the assertion could become unsound.
evidence: acquiredBudget is only set true at line 422, which is inside `if (this.coreBudget)`. So when acquiredBudget is true, coreBudget is provably non-null. The non-null assertion is correct. TypeScript's control flow doesn't track this across the async boundary but the runtime logic is sound. The finder's self-reported confidence is 'low.'

title: forEachSubscriber always spreads the subscriber Set into a new array, allocating on every message dispatch
description: forEachSubscriber is called on every incoming worker message to fan out to dedupe subscribers. It always does `[...subs]` to snapshot the Set before iterating, allocating a new Array on every call.
code_excerpt: for (const sub of [...subs]) fn(sub);
evidence: Line 157 of dedupe.ts confirms the spread on every call. The comment explains the spread is required for correctness (fn may mutate the Set mid-iteration). A size===0 guard before the spread would eliminate allocations for sessions with no fan-out subscribers, which is the common case. The spread is O(1) for size=1 (primary only) but still allocates.

title: abortAcquisition calls dedupe.subscribers() which spreads to array, should use forEachSubscriber
description: abortAcquisition calls `this.dedupe.subscribers(params.sessionId)` which spreads to a new array, then immediately iterates it. The subscribers() method is marked `@internal — prefer forEachSubscriber`.
code_excerpt:
const subs = this.dedupe.subscribers(params.sessionId);
for (const sub of subs) { ... }
evidence: Line 270 of scheduler.ts calls dedupe.subscribers() which does [...Set] (dedupe.ts line 143). dedupe.ts line 139 marks subscribers() as `@internal — prefer forEachSubscriber for bounded-allocation iteration`. The allocation is unnecessary on the abort path and the fix is a one-line replacement.

title: signalDrain calls adaptiveHwm() twice per invocation
description: adaptiveHwm() performs a floating-point multiply, min/max, and Math.round on every call. In signalDrain it is called at line 694 to assign `const hwm` but the while-loop at line 697 calls `this.adaptiveHwm()` again.
evidence: Reading lines 694-697 carefully: line 694 assigns `const hwm = this.adaptiveHwm()` and line 697 uses `hwm` (the const) in the while-loop condition, not `this.adaptiveHwm()`. The while loop guard reads `bp.queueDepth < hwm` — the cached value. The finder misread the code; adaptiveHwm() is only called once per signalDrain invocation.

title: handleWorkerMessage calls forEachSubscriber on every message even when no dedupe is active
description: Every worker message unconditionally calls `this.dedupe.forEachSubscriber(sessionId, ...)`. For non-deduped sessions (the majority), sessionToSubscribers returns a Set containing only the primary itself; the spread still allocates a 1-element array.
code_excerpt: this.dedupe.forEachSubscriber(sessionId, (subId) => { ... });
evidence: Line 1204 calls forEachSubscriber unconditionally. Since every primary registers itself as a subscriber (dedupe.ts line 29), sessionToSubscribers always has at least the primary ID, so the spread always allocates a 1-element array even for non-deduped sessions. A hasSubscribers() fast-path guard would eliminate this allocation on the majority of messages.

title: idleWorkers and activeWorkers getters spread Sets into arrays on every call
description: The `idleWorkers` and `activeWorkers` getters each spread the underlying Set into a new readonly array on every property access.
code_excerpt: get idleWorkers(): readonly PoolWorker[] { return [...this.idle]; }
evidence: Lines 133-140 already provide zero-allocation idleWorkerValues() and activeWorkerValues() iterators with an explicit comment 'Zero-allocation iterator for hot scheduler paths.' The hot path in scheduler.ts uses these iterators. The getter-with-spread is intentionally for safe external/diagnostic use, not hot paths. No performance bug exists — the right tool for hot paths is already provided.

title: findBackgroundWorker calls dedupe.forEachSubscriber (with Set spread) for every background worker candidate
description: findBackgroundWorker iterates backgroundWorkers and for each candidate calls `dedupe.forEachSubscriber(worker.activeSessionId, ...)` which spreads the subscriber Set.
evidence: Lines 1039-1043 call forEachSubscriber per background worker candidate. In steady-state (no fan-out), each call spreads a 1-element Set. A hasVisibleSubscriber(primaryId) boolean helper on DedupeRegistry could check without spreading. The allocation is N per preemption attempt where N is the number of background workers.

title: No micro-benchmark or allocation-count CI exists for the hot handleWorkerMessage dispatch path
description: The scheduler's handleWorkerMessage is the hottest path. There are no benchmarks measuring per-message dispatch cost or allocation count.
code_excerpt: private handleWorkerMessage(sessionId: string, worker: PoolWorker, rawMsg: WorkerToMainMessage): void {
evidence: No benchmark files exist under packages/jxl-scheduler/. The handleWorkerMessage path is called for every decoded frame and drain signal, making it the performance-critical path. Changes to fan-out or deduplication could silently regress throughput. The opportunity is real.

title: acquireSlot returns workerId=-1 for dedupe subscribers whose primary has no worker yet
description: When a session is a dedupe subscriber and the primary is still queued, acquireSlot returns `{ workerId: -1 }`. The declared return type has no documented sentinel.
code_excerpt: return { workerId: primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1 };
evidence: Confirmed by reading line 334. The -1 sentinel is undocumented in the return type. contracts-2 further shows the queued path's resolve closure also yields -1 if read before assignWorker runs (though the async path at lines 1374-1379 calls assignWorker before pending.resolve(), making that path safe). The subscriber path on line 334 is the primary concern.

title: Queued-path acquireSlot resolve closure reads workerId at call time, not at assignment time
description: The queued path captures the resolve callback as a closure over `this.sessions.get(params.sessionId)?.worker?.id ?? -1`. resolve() is called from drainQueue after assignWorker.
code_excerpt: resolve: () => resolve({ workerId: this.sessions.get(params.sessionId)?.worker?.id ?? -1 }),
evidence: The finder's own analysis (final sentence) correctly concludes the async drainQueue path calls assignWorker (line 1374) before pending.resolve() (line 1379), so the session record already has worker set when the closure executes. The closure re-reads sessions.get() at resolve() time (not at enqueue time), so it reads the worker after assignment. The -1 fallback would only fire if the session was somehow removed before resolve() — which would be a different bug. The queued path is correct.

title: acquireSlot does not validate that sessionId is a non-empty string or that it differs from RESERVED_SESSION_ID
description: Passing `sessionId='__jxl_reserved__'` would register a session under the reserved sentinel value, corrupting the pool's activeSessionId guard in `ensureWorkerWired`.
code_excerpt: async acquireSlot(params: { sessionId: string; ... })
evidence: RESERVED_SESSION_ID is '__jxl_reserved__' (pool.ts line 20). ensureWorkerWired (line 1149+) routes messages by comparing worker.activeSessionId to session IDs. A session registered as '__jxl_reserved__' would bind to any RESERVED_SESSION_ID worker and cause messages for that worker to be silently dropped by the RESERVED_SESSION_ID filter. The guard is a trivial one-line check.

title: cancelSession with promotion does not correctly adjust counters when promoted subscriber was in running state but held no worker
description: When a primary is cancelled and a subscriber is promoted, adjustSessionCount(record, -1) removes the primary from its bucket, and the isSubscriber block increments the appropriate bucket for the promoted record. However if the promoted record has state='running' (subscribers always start as running, line 320), _runningCount++ fires for a session that holds no worker.
code_excerpt: if (promotedRecord.state === "running" || promotedRecord.state === "cancelling") this._runningCount++;
evidence: Line 575 increments _runningCount when promotedRecord.state === 'running'. Subscriber records always have state='running' (line 320) but hold no worker. The primary's adjustSessionCount(-1) at line 571 decrements the primary's bucket (e.g. _runningCount for a running primary). Net result: if both primary and subscriber were 'running', _runningCount stays the same — but the promoted session now holds the worker, so the count is semantically correct. However if the primary was 'queued' or 'paused', adjustSessionCount decrements _queuedCount or _pausedCount, and the isSubscriber block increments _runningCount for the promoted subscriber — this correctly transitions the count from queued/paused into running. The real concern from logic-3 stands: when the queued transfer path sets promotedRecord.state='queued', _queuedCount gets incremented for a record that was previously in _subscriberCount (not _queuedCount), overcounting _queuedCount.

title: Synthesized decode_cancelled on preemption timeout may double-deliver if a genuine terminal arrives after worker recycle
description: In tryPreempt timeout path, a synthesized decode_cancelled is sent to victimHandlers and cleanupSession is called. If the recycled worker later emits a genuine decode_cancelled, ensureWorkerWired may route it to handleWorkerMessage which calls cleanupSession again.
code_excerpt:
for (const h of victimHandlers) { try { h(cancelMsg); } catch {} }
this.cleanupSession(victimSessionId);
evidence: cleanupSession calls sessions.delete(sessionId) (line 1303). After the first cleanupSession, the session record is gone. When the recycled worker's genuine terminal arrives, ensureWorkerWired (line 1149) checks worker.activeSessionId. pool.recycle() calls cleanupAndRemove which sets activeSessionId=null — so any subsequent message from the recycled worker has sessionId null, which will not match any session and will be dropped before reaching handleWorkerMessage. Double-cleanup cannot occur.

title: completeSession does not validate that sessionId belongs to a primary; calling it on a subscriber tears down subscriber without notifying primary
description: completeSession unconditionally calls cleanupSession then drainQueue. cleanupSession calls dedupe.complete(sessionId), which for a subscriber only detaches the subscriber. The primary continues running with a stale subscriber count.
evidence: dedupe.complete() on a subscriber (line 117-123 of dedupe.ts) only calls subscriberToPrimary.delete and removes the subscriber from the primary's Set — it does not notify the primary or decrement the primary's subscriber count tracked in the scheduler. The scheduler's _subscriberCount is decremented by cleanupSession only if the record.isSubscriber flag is set. If a caller invokes completeSession on a subscriber, the subscriber is cleaned up but _subscriberCount is decremented correctly (cleanupSession uses adjustSessionCount which checks isSubscriber). However, the primary's sessionToSubscribers Set loses the entry, meaning future fan-out messages skip this subscriber without knowing it was force-completed. The partial teardown is a real correctness gap.

title: isTerminalMessage does not include decode_paused, causing stale paused-ack to be dispatched to handlers without cleanup
description: `decode_paused` is not in `isTerminalMessage`. A stale decode_paused arriving on the normal message handler path would be dispatched to handlers but not treated as terminal.
evidence: decode_paused is NOT a terminal message — it is an intermediate ack in the preemption protocol, consumed by the ackHandlers map (tryPreempt's promise). decode_paused should not be in isTerminalMessage. If a stale decode_paused arrives on the normal path after preemption is complete, it correctly flows to handlers (which may ignore it) without triggering session cleanup. Adding decode_paused to isTerminalMessage would incorrectly terminate the session when a paused ack arrives out-of-order.

title: DedupeRegistry.register does not guard against re-registering the same sessionId
description: Calling register(sessionId, newKey) when sessionId is already registered overwrites sessionToKey without removing the old keyToSession entry.
evidence: register() has no guard against re-registration. If called twice for the same sessionId with different keys, the old keyToSession entry is not removed — a stale key still points to the session. In normal usage the scheduler calls register() only once per new primary (after findPrimary returns null), but the contract is unenforced on DedupeRegistry itself, making it fragile to refactors.

title: protectMetricForDispatch only clones type=metric messages; non-metric messages with mutable sub-objects are passed by reference to all handlers
description: For non-metric message types, protectMetricForDispatch returns the original msg object reference. All handlers receive the exact same object. If any handler mutates a field, subsequent handlers see the mutated object.
evidence: Lines 796-801 confirm the passthrough for non-metric messages. decode_budget_exceeded carries a pixels ArrayBuffer and is dispatched to all primary and subscriber handlers by reference. A handler that sets msg.pixels=null after reading would corrupt subsequent handlers. The CLAUDE.md contract says postMessage detaches ArrayBuffers — if a handler processes pixels in a way that modifies the message object, downstream handlers see the mutated state.

title: scheduler.admission.test.ts is a stub with no tests, leaving AdmissionGate contract completely untested
description: The file contains only a single comment. Zero actual test cases for AdmissionGate behavior.
code_excerpt: /* sched-2 admission test - see previous session for full cases. Core impl in scheduler.ts + types.ts */
evidence: The file is confirmed to contain only a single comment (read directly). AdmissionGate gate blocking, cancellation during admit(), and gateReleases cleanup have no test coverage. Given that errors-11 confirms a real hang risk if admit() stalls, the missing tests represent a concrete gap.

title: WorkerHandle and AdmissionGate interfaces have no version discriminant; adding required fields is a silent breaking change
description: The public interfaces WorkerHandle, WorkerFactory, and AdmissionGate carry no version marker. Adding a required method would silently break all implementations.
evidence: The interfaces are implemented by jxl-worker-browser/spawn.ts and jxl-worker-node/spawn.ts. Adding a required field would break both at runtime without a compile-time catch if those packages are built separately. An optional capability-probe method would allow graceful degradation. The opportunity is genuine for a library used by multiple packages.

title: After pause ack, park+unpark bypasses normal pool lifecycle, leaving worker untracked between unpark() and bind()
description: After successful decode_paused ack, pool.park() then pool.unpark() are called back-to-back then activeSessionId is set manually to RESERVED_SESSION_ID. The worker is not in idle/active/parked but is in workers map during the window between unpark and bind.
evidence: Confirmed by reading pool.ts: park() moves to parked set (active.delete, idle.delete, parked.add, activeSessionId=null). unpark() calls parked.delete only. After unpark, the worker is in workers but not in idle/active/parked — assertInvariants would fail if called here. Then line 954 sets activeSessionId=RESERVED_SESSION_ID manually bypassing reserve(). The subsequent pool.bind() at line 974 (via assignWorker) checks activeSessionId===RESERVED_SESSION_ID and calls transitionToActive, which calls idle.delete (no-op) and active.add — this works but the reserve() guard that normally prevents double-bind is bypassed by the manual RESERVED_SESSION_ID assignment.

title: SchedulerOptions has no runtime validation; invalid combinations produce degenerate scheduler behavior
description: SchedulerOptions accepts any numeric values for maxWorkers, pushHwm, etc. A maxWorkers=0 produces a scheduler that can never acquire a worker but does not throw at construction.
evidence: No validation of SchedulerOptions values exists in the Scheduler constructor. maxWorkers=0 would set pool.maxSize=0, causing acquire() to always return null (line 203: totalAllocatedOrSpawning >= maxSize). The scheduler silently becomes a no-op. A construction-time validation throwing for maxWorkers <= 0 would surface the misconfiguration immediately.
